title: Was Marx Right? Evaluating Marxist Political Economy

sections.title: 1. Why Ask ‘Was Marx Right?’
sections.content: This section introduces the guiding question of the session: what does it mean to evaluate whether Marx was ‘right’? Rather than treating Marxism as a fixed doctrine, students are encouraged to see Marx as a political economist making historical and theoretical claims about capitalism. The section explains why Marx remains influential in political economy, sociology, and contemporary debates about inequality, globalization, and crises. It clarifies that ‘being right’ can refer to different dimensions: diagnosis of capitalism, predictions about its development, or the usefulness of Marx’s analytical tools today.

sections.title: 2. Core Elements of Marx’s Political Economy
sections.content: This section outlines the foundational concepts of Marx’s critique of capitalism. It explains historical materialism as a method that links economic structures to social and political life. Key ideas include the labor theory of value, surplus value, exploitation, and class relations between capital and labor. The section emphasizes Marx’s focus on capitalism as a dynamic system driven by competition, accumulation, and profit-seeking, rather than simply a market mechanism.

sections.title: 3. Marx on Capitalism’s Dynamics and Contradictions
sections.content: Here the content explores Marx’s analysis of how capitalism develops over time. It covers concepts such as capital accumulation, technological change, the tendency toward concentration and centralization of capital, and recurring economic crises. The section explains Marx’s argument that capitalism generates internal contradictions—such as rising productivity alongside worker insecurity—that periodically destabilize the system.

sections.title: 4. Marx’s Expectations and Predictions
sections.content: This section examines some of Marx’s most debated claims, including the immiseration of the working class, the tendency of the rate of profit to fall, and the expectation that capitalism would eventually be replaced by a different mode of production. It stresses the difference between deterministic predictions and conditional tendencies, encouraging students to read Marx carefully rather than as a simple prophet of collapse.

sections.title: 5. Capitalism Since Marx: Evidence and Challenges
sections.content: This section places Marx’s ideas in historical perspective by looking at developments since the 19th century. Topics include industrialization, welfare states, organized labor, globalization, and financialization. The section highlights areas where capitalism has proven more adaptable than Marx anticipated, as well as areas—such as inequality and crisis—where his analysis still appears relevant.

sections.title: 6. Major Critiques of Marxist Political Economy
sections.content: This section introduces key criticisms from alternative traditions in political economy. These include neoclassical critiques of the labor theory of value, Weberian and institutional critiques of economic determinism, and arguments that Marx underplayed the role of politics, culture, and the state. The section emphasizes that evaluating Marx requires engaging with these critiques rather than ignoring them.

sections.title: 7. Contemporary Uses of Marx Today
sections.content: This section explores how Marx’s ideas are used in contemporary scholarship and debate. It discusses neo-Marxist and heterodox approaches that adapt Marxian concepts to analyze globalization, platform capitalism, precarious work, and environmental crises. The focus is on Marx as a source of analytical tools rather than a closed system of answers.

sections.title: 8. How to Evaluate ‘Was Marx Right?’
sections.content: The final section provides a framework for student reflection. It distinguishes between Marx as a theorist of capitalism, a historical analyst, and a political thinker. Students are encouraged to weigh strengths and limitations, consider context, and reflect on whether Marx’s concepts help them understand contemporary political economy. The emphasis is on critical evaluation rather than arriving at a single correct verdict.

examples: Using the concept of surplus value to explain how profits are generated in a factory setting.
Applying Marx’s idea of concentration of capital to modern multinational corporations.
Comparing Marx’s expectations about worker immiseration with the development of welfare states.
Using Marxian analysis to interpret the 2008 global financial crisis.
Applying Marx’s framework to contemporary debates about gig work and platform economies.
